S30: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. A B S T R A C T Background Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is a progressive neurodegenerative disease without eﬀective therapies.
S31: Several studies have suggested that repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) may have positive benefit in ALS.
S32: However, the eﬀicacy and safety of this therapy remain uncertain.
S33: This is the first update of a review published in 2011.
S34: Objectives To determine the clinical eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS for treating ALS.
S35: Search methods On 30 July 2012, we searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register, CENTRAL (2012, issue 7 in The Cochrane Library), MEDLINE (1966 to July 2012), EMBASE (1980 to July 2012), CINAHL (1937 to July 2012), Science Citation Index Expanded (January 1945 to July 2012), AMED (January 1985 to July 2012).
S36: We searched the Chinese Biomedical Database (1979 to August 2012).
S37: We also searched for ongoing studies on clinicaltrials.gov (August 2012).
S38: Selection criteria Randomised and quasi-randomised controlled trials assessing the therapeutic eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS for patients with a clinical diagnosis of ALS.
S39: Comparisons eligible for inclusion were: 1. rTMS versus no intervention; 2. rTMS versus sham rTMS; 3. rTMS versus physiotherapy; 4. rTMS versus medications; 5. rTMS + other therapies or drugs versus sham rTMS + the same therapies or drugs; 6. diﬀerent methods of application of rTMS such as high-frequency (> 1Hz) compared to low-frequency (≤ 1Hz) rTMS.

S44: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Data collection and analysis Two authors independently selected papers, assessed risk of bias and extracted data.
S46: We contacted study authors for additional information.
S47: Main results Three randomised, placebo-controlled trials with a total of 50 participants were included in the review.
S48: All three trials compared rTMS with sham TMS.
S49: All the trials were of poor methodological quality and were insuﬀiciently homogeneous to allow the pooling of results.
S50: Moreover, the high rate of attrition further increased the risk of bias.
S51: None of the trials provided detailed data on the ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised (ALSFRS-R) scores at six months follow-up which was pre-assigned as our primary outcome.
S52: One trial contained data in a suitable form for quantitative analysis of our secondary outcomes.
S53: No diﬀerence was seen between rTMS and sham rTMS using the ALSFRS- R scores and manual muscle testing (MMT) scores at 12 months follow-up in this trial.
S54: Additionally, none of the trials reported any adverse events associated with the use of rTMS.
S55: However, in view of the small sample size, the methodological limitations and incomplete outcome data, treatment with rTMS cannot be judged as completely safe.
S56: Authors' conclusions There is currently insuﬀicient evidence to draw conclusions about the eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS in the treatment of ALS.
S57: Further studies may be helpful if their potential benefit is weighed against the impact of participation in a randomised controlled trial on people with ALS.
S58: P L A I N L A N G U A G E S U M M A R Y Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) for treating amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), which is also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is a fatal disease in which the nerves in the brain and spinal cord that control movement degenerate.
S59: Treatments have little eﬀect on how the disease progresses.
S60: People with ALS develop muscle weakness and paralysis of limb muscles and muscles involved in swallowing and breathing.
S61: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a method for exciting nerve cells in superficial areas of the brain.
S62: It applies pulsed magnetic fields to the surface of the brain via an electrode on the scalp.
S63: There have been trials to see if rTMS is eﬀective in people with ALS.
S64: For this review we searched widely for clinical trials of rTMS in people with ALS and found three studies, which involved 50 participants in total.
S65: All three compared rTMS with sham (inactive) rTMS.
S66: None of the three studies reported on disability or limitation in activity as assessed by a specific ALS scale (ALSFRS-R) at six months follow-up, which was what we chose as our primary measure of the eﬀectiveness of rTMS.
S67: Other outcome measures were only available from 12 participants in one poor quality trial, in which there was no diﬀerence between rTMS and sham rTMS in ALSFRS-R or a test of muscle strength at 12 months’ follow-up.
S68: None of the studies reported any adverse eﬀects with rTMS.
S69: The trials in this review had small numbers of participants and some problems of design, so larger, well-designed trials should be considered, to determine the eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS in ALS.
S70: However, the potential benefit should be balanced against the impact of taking part in trials for people with ALS.

S99: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews B A C K G R O U N D Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is one of the major neurodegenerative diseases alongside Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease.
S100: It is an adult-onset neurodegenerative disorder that causes premature loss of motor neurons at all levels, including the cerebral cortex, brainstem and spinal cord, and gives rise to both upper and lower motor neuron signs.
S101: Description of the condition ALS is considered a rare disease worldwide, with a reported incidence between 1.5 and 2.0 per 100,000 population per year and a prevalence of 1 per 20,000 (Mandrioli 2003; Sorenson 2002).
S102: The average age of ALS onset is 55 years and males are usually aﬀected more than females (ratio about 1.6:1).
S103: Five to ten per cent of patients have a positive family history for ALS.
S104: These families usually show an autosomal-dominant pattern of inheritance.
S105: The other 90% of patients without a family history are said to have sporadic disease, the cause of which is unknown.
S106: The clinical features of ALS can be considered in relation to neurological regions.
S107: Bulbar-onset patients ofen present with dysarthria, dysphagia, or both.
S108: Cervical-onset patients ofen present with muscle atrophy and upper-limb weakness, associated with brisk reflexes.
S109: Lumbar-onset patients always have lower motor neuron symptoms and signs in the legs, such as a tendency to trip (distal weakness) or diﬀiculty on stairs (proximal weakness).
S110: Death occurs in most patients within two to five years of diagnosis, usually from respiratory failure caused by ventilatory muscle weakness (Forsgren 1983; Rowland 2001).
S111: While the aetiology of ALS is unknown, current evidence suggests that multiple interacting factors contribute to motor neuron injury in ALS.
S112: One of the hypothesised pathogenetic mechanisms is glutamate-driven excitotoxicity in the motor cortex (Zanette 2002).
S113: This mechanism provides a rationale for clinical trials which have demonstrated that riluzole, as a glutamate antagonist, improves clinical outcomes in patients with ALS (Miller 2007).
S114: However, some other treatments may also have a treatment eﬀect in this condition (Andersen 2005).
S115: Description of the intervention Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) is a non-invasive means of stimulating nerve cells in superficial areas of the brain by applying a high-energy magnetic field at the skull surface which induces a perpendicular electrical field in the vertical plane through the cortex.
S116: It was first introduced by Barker et al.
S118: Since then, numerous studies have found that TMS provides a non-invasive approach to condition the excitability and activity of neurons (Kobayashi 2003; Siebner 2003) and can be used as a tool to evaluate the motor system and study the function of brain regions.
S119: Moreover, some clinical trials have shown that TMS might be eﬀective in treating various diseases, including Parkinson's disease, epilepsy, dystonia depression and schizophrenia (Borich 2009; Elahi 2009; Fitzgerald 2003; Klein 1999; Theodore 2002).
S120: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a type of TMS that occurs in a rhythmic and repetitive form.
S121: If the stimulation occurs at a frequency equal to or less than once per second (1 Hz) it is called low-frequency rTMS and if the speed of stimulation is faster than 1 Hz it is called high-frequency rTMS (Wassermann 1998).
S122: Some previous studies have demonstrated that low-frequency rTMS produces a decrease of motor cortex excitability lasting up to 30 minutes post stimulation (Chen 1997; Plewnia 2003; Romero 2002) and brings a reduction in glutamate-induced excitotoxicity, which may benefit people with ALS (Ziemann 2004).
S123: Conversely, high-frequency rTMS provokes a short-term increase in cortical excitability (Di Lazarro 2002), which may be detrimental in ALS.
S124: However, high-frequency rTMS may have neuroprotective eﬀects (Fujiki 2003) and increase the expression of neurotrophic factors such as brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) (Angelucci 2004), which could counteract the neuronal damage in ALS.
S125: These experimental findings suggest that both low- and high-frequency rTMS might be eﬀective therapies for ALS.
S126: Diﬀerent rTMS protocols have been developed.
S127: The theta burst magnetic stimulation (TBS) of the human motor cortex, a kind of novel rTMS technique, which can swifly produce powerful and controllable long-term changes in the excitability of cortical circuits, is widely used nowadays (Huang 2004).
S128: The basic TBS pattern is of a train containing three pulses at 50 Hz delivered every 200 ms. On the basis of this, two diﬀerent paradigms of TBS with diﬀerent eﬀects on corticospinal excitability are employed: continuous TBS (cTBS, a 40 second train of three pulses of 50 Hz stimulation repeated every 200 ms for a total of 600 stimuli) and intermittent TBS (iTBS, 10 bursts of high frequency stimulation, three pulses at 50 Hz, are applied at 5 Hz every 10 s for a total of 600 pulses) (Huang 2004).
S129: As cTBS can lead to a marked depression of cortical excitability and a significant reduction of glutamatergic- related intracortical facilitation (Huang 2005), some studies have adopted cTBS as a therapeutic tool to aid the treatment of ALS (Di Lazarro 2006; Di Lazarro 2009; Di Lazarro 2010).
S130: This limited evidence has suggested that this kind of rTMS may have a positive benefit in ALS.
S131: Therefore, this review will consider rTMS as a therapeutic intervention for ALS.
S132: Why it is important to do this review In recent years, several clinical trials have been conducted to investigate the eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS in treating ALS.
S133: The initial results of these studies have suggested that rTMS could improve motor function in ALS (Di Lazarro 2006; Zanette 2008), while other studies have demonstrated that rTMS may worsen the clinical course of the disease (Di Lazarro 2004).
S134: However, we know of no systematic review of rTMS for the treatment of ALS.
S135: For this reason, a systematic review to assess the benefits and harms of rTMS for ALS is needed.
S136: We therefore planned to perform a systematic review which would be based on evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and quasi-randomised controlled trials (quasi-RCTs) that examine the eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS for ALS.
S138: This review has a published protocol (He 2010).
S139: O B J E C T I V E S The primary objective of the review was to examine the eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS in the treatment of patients with ALS.

S144: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews M E T H O D S Criteria for considering studies for this review Types of studies We included RCTs and quasi-RCTs assessing the therapeutic eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS for patients with ALS.
S145: Types of participants We included participants of any age or sex with a clinical diagnosis of definite, probable, probable with laboratory support or possible ALS according to the El Escorial criteria.
S147: Types of interventions (a) We carried out comparisons of: 1. rTMS versus no intervention; 2. rTMS versus sham rTMS; 3. rTMS versus physiotherapy; 4. rTMS versus medications; 5. rTMS + other therapies or drugs versus sham rTMS + the same therapies or drugs.
S148: (b) We also compared diﬀerent methods of application of rTMS such as high-frequency (> 1Hz) compared to low-frequency (≤ 1Hz) rTMS.
S149: Types of outcome measures Primary outcomes The primary outcome focused on disability or limitation in activity as assessed by the ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised (ALSFRS-R) (Cedarbaum 1999) at six months.
S150: Secondary outcomes Secondary outcome measures included: 1.
S151: Changes to the ALSFRS-R scores at 12 months or longer follow- up.
S153: Changes to muscle strength as measured by manual muscle testing (MMT) at one month and six months or longer follow-up.
S155: Changes to fatigue as measured by the Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS) (Krupp 1989) at one month and six months or longer follow- up.
S157: Adverse events that may have resulted from the intervention.
S158: We defined serious adverse events as life-threatening conditions, prolonged hospitalisation or death.
S159: Nonserious adverse events included: tinnitus; dizziness; and progression of ALS-related weakness beyond that expected as a result of typical disease progression, with events leading to cessation of treatment.
S160: Search methods for identification of studies Electronic searches On 30 July 2012, we searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register (30 July 2012), CENTRAL (2012, issue 7 in The Cochrane Library), MEDLINE (1966 to July 2012), EMBASE (1980 to July 2012), CINAHL (1937 to July 2012), ISI (Science Citation Index Expanded, January 1945 to July 2012), AMED (the Allied and Complementary Medicine Database, January 1985 to July 2012), We also searched the Chinese Biomedical Database (1979 to August 2012) and www.ClinicalTrials.gov (http://clinicaltrials.gov/) (August 2012).
S161: The detailed search strategies are in the appendices: MEDLINE (Appendix 1), EMBASE (Appendix 2), AMED (Appendix 3), CINAHL Plus (Appendix 4), CENTRAL (Appendix 5), ISI (Appendix 6), Chinese Biomedical Database (Appendix 7) and www.ClinicalTrials.gov (Appendix 8).
S162: We screened search results for RCTs as well as well- designed published observational studies.
S163: Searching other resources We checked the reference lists of published studies to identify more trials.
S164: We also searched for unpublished data and grey literature through personal communication with researchers and others with an interest in the field, and contacted the corresponding authors of identified RCTs for additional information about other relevant studies.
S165: We did not include any restrictions with respect to language.
S166: Data collection and analysis Selection of studies Two authors (Jian Guo (JG) and MY for the original review, JF and MY at this update) independently scrutinised the titles, abstract sections, and keywords of every record retrieved to determine if the studies had to be assessed further.
S167: Two authors (JG or JF, MY) obtained and assessed full copies of potentially relevant trials as per the 'Criteria for considering studies for this review'.
S168: The authors decided which trials fitted the inclusion criteria and graded their risk of bias.
S169: There were no disagreements about inclusion.
S170: Data extraction and management Two review authors (JG or JF, CZ) independently extracted data on participants, methods, interventions, outcomes and results using a data extraction form.
S171: Where data were missing, we contacted the study authors via e-mail and requested this information.
S172: One review author (JG or JF) entered data into the Cochrane Review Manager (RevMan) 5 sofware (RevMan 2008, updated to RevMan 2012), and two other review authors (MY, MZ) checked the data entered.
S173: We resolved disagreements on data extraction by discussion.
S174: Assessment of risk of bias in included studies Two review authors (JG, MZ for the original review) independently assessed the risk of bias in each included study and completed the 'Risk of bias' table according to the guidance in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins 2011).
S175: JF and MZ reassessed blinding for this update.
S176: The following six methodological domains were considered for each trial.

S195: We made a judgement on each of these criteria relating to the risk of bias of 'Low risk', 'High risk' or 'Unclear risk'.
S196: We resolved disagreements by discussion with a third author (CZ).
S197: Measures of treatment eﬀect We used the Cochrane statistical sofware RevMan 5 to measure the treatment eﬀect according to the recommendations of the Cochrane Collaboration.
S198: We calculated the weighted treatment eﬀect using a fixed-eﬀect model across trials.
S199: Results were expressed as risk ratios (RRs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs), risk diﬀerences (RDs) with 95% CIs for dichotomous outcomes and as mean diﬀerences (MDs) with 95% CIs for continuous outcomes.
S200: We constructed a 'Summary of findings' table using GRADEpro sofware and included the following outcomes.
S202: Changes to the ALSFRS-R scores at 12 months follow-up.
S204: Changes to the MMT scores at 12 months follow-up.
S206: Changes to FSS at 12 months follow-up.
S208: Adverse events leading to cessation of treatment.
S209: Dealing with missing data We planned to perform analyses on an intention-to-treat basis whenever possible.
S210: We included all participants with available data in the analysis in the group to which they were allocated, regardless of whether or not they received the allocated intervention.
S211: If in the original reports participants were not analysed in the group to which they were randomised, and there was suﬀicient information in the trial report, we attempted to restore them to the correct group.
S212: We dealt with missing data through an attempt to contact the study authors via email.
S213: Assessment of heterogeneity We used the Chi2 test for heterogeneity to provide an indication of between-study heterogeneity.
S214: In addition, we quantified the degree of heterogeneity observed in the results using the I2 statistic with significance being set at P < 0.1 (Higgins 2002; Higgins 2003).
S215: We planned to assess possible sources of heterogeneity by sensitivity and subgroup analyses as described below.
S216: For trials that were clinically heterogeneous or presented insuﬀicient information for pooling, we planned to perform a descriptive analysis (Higgins 2011).
S217: Assessment of reporting biases As there were insuﬀicient studies included in the review, we used a descriptive analysis to evaluate the potential reporting biases.
S218: Data synthesis We planned to perform meta-analysis using RevMan 5 sofware if trials were clinically similar enough; otherwise we would analyse the trials separately.
S219: Subgroup analysis and investigation of heterogeneity We planned to perform subgroup analyses in order to explore eﬀect size diﬀerence according to the following methodological sources of heterogeneity if suﬀicient trials specifying these parameters were available.
S220: (1) State of ALS at baseline: mild, moderate, severe or terminal (Riviere 1998).
S221: (2) Clinical pattern of the disease: bulbar onset or limb onset.
S222: (3) Type of TMS: high-frequency (> 1Hz) or low-frequency (≤ 1Hz) stimulation.
S223: Sensitivity analysis If heterogeneity was found across the trials, we intended to undertake sensitivity analyses in order to determine the eﬀect of omitting trials with a high risk of bias.
S224: R E S U L T S Description of studies Results of the search The number of possibly relevant studies found on each database were as follows: Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Register: 5 records; the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL): 24 records; MEDLINE: 76 records; EMBASE: 239 records; CINAHL plus: 16 records; AMED: 1 record; Science Citation Index Expanded: 40 records; the Chinese Biomedical Database: 0 records; Clinicaltrials.gov: 1 record.

S227: Six trials were excluded afer we screened the full text of all the possible relevant references and three met the inclusion criteria.

S229: For the 2013 update we did not find any new trials for inclusion.

S231: Included studies Trial location and participants All three included trials were conducted in a single centre in Italy (Di Lazarro 2006; Di Lazarro 2009; Zanette 2008).
S232: Of the three included trials, two were carried out by the same group of researchers investigating the eﬀects of rTMS on the progression of ALS (Di Lazarro 2006; Di Lazarro 2009).
S233: Both trials had similar design, intervention, eﬀicacy and safety assessments.
S234: Baseline demographic and clinical characteristics of the participants in the included trials are shown in Table 1.
S235: The included trials had 50 participants with ALS ranging from 5 to 70 months duration.
S236: The mean age of the intervention group ranged from 59.4 to 60.6 years and control group ranged from 55.1 to 65.7 years.
S237: The ratio of males to females in intervention group ranged from 2.5 to 4 and control group ranged from 0.6 to 2.3.
S238: Two studies included in the review recruited patients who fulfilled the EI Escorial criteria for definite or probable ALS (Di Lazarro 2009; Zanette 2008) and one study only recruited patients with criteria for definite ALS (Di Lazarro 2006).

S245: Interventions All the three trials were randomised, parallel-group trials designed to test the eﬀectiveness of active rTMS versus sham rTMS in reducing the progression of ALS.
S246: In two studies, the participants and assessors were blinded to the treatment assignment (Di Lazarro 2006; Di Lazarro 2009 ).
S247: In one study, it was not stated whether the assessors were blinded (Zanette 2008).
S248: Table 2 lists the rTMS parameters used in the three included studies.
S249: Among the three randomised controlled studies there was heterogeneity in the rTMS technique with respect to three variables: duration of treatment, frequency of rTMS and intensity of rTMS.
S250: The study by Di Lazarro 2006 delivered rTMS for five consecutive days per month for six months to the motor cortex of each hemisphere.
S251: The stimulation protocol was cTBS, in which three pulses of stimulation are given at 50 Hz, repeated every 200 ms for a total of 600 pulses.
S252: The stimulus intensity was 80% of action motor threshold.
S253: Another of the included trials carried out subsequently by the same group delivered rTMS for five consecutive days per month for 12 months, using the same stimulation protocol (Di Lazarro 2009).
S254: The study by Zanette 2008 delivered the rTMS for five consecutive days per week for two weeks.
S255: Stimulation protocol was 20 trains of 15 stimuli at 5 Hz, 60 s interval between trains.
S256: The stimulus intensity was 110% resting motor threshold.
S257: Outcomes The primary outcome of all the three trials was rate of decline of ALSFRS-R scores.
S258: But time points linked to the primary outcome measures were heterogeneous: four weeks in Zanette 2008, six months in Di Lazarro 2006 and six months and 12 months in Di Lazarro 2009.
S259: Two studies used the rate of decline of MMT as their secondary outcome (performed by means of the Medical Research Council (MRC) Scale) afer the end of rTMS treatment (Di Lazarro 2006; Di Lazarro 2009 ), while another study used the changes in muscle strength (assessed by the MRC scale) and fatigue (assessed by Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS)) afer the treatment as the secondary outcome (Zanette 2008).
S260: All studies clearly reviewed and recorded serious adverse events and events leading to discontinuations.
S261: Excluded studies Six studies were excluded from the analysis of this review.
S262: For full details see Characteristics of excluded studies.
S263: One study was not a randomised trial and measured diﬀerent outcomes from our protocol (Di Lazarro 2004).
S264: One study was a research report to investigate the eﬀects of rTMS on BDNF plasma levels in ALS patients (Angelucci 2004).
S265: One study enrolled only two participants (Di Lazarro 2010).
S266: Two studies investigated rTMS measures as clinical correlates and longitudinal markers of ALS (Floyd 2009; Khedr 2011).The last report was a review of rTMS as a treatment for ALS (Dileone 2010).
S267: Risk of bias in included studies Overall, there was little information on methods provided by study authors.
S268: For full details, see Characteristics of included studies and 'Risk of bias' summary (Figure 1).

S341: Adverse events that may have resulted from the intervention Treatment appeared safe in all included studies.
S342: There was no report of any adverse events related to rTMS.

S344: D I S C U S S I O N The aim of this review was to examine the eﬀectiveness and safety of rTMS for the treatment of ALS.
S345: The evidence from the randomised controlled trials (RCTs) included in our review does not support rTMS as a treatment for patients with ALS.
S346: All the trials we identified were designed to compare real rTMS versus sham rTMS treatment.
S347: We did not find any trials reporting comparisons with other therapies or drugs, nor any trials comparing high-frequency rTMS to low-frequency rTMS.
S348: All the three included studies had relatively small sample sizes, 20 in the Di Lazarro 2006, 20 in the Di Lazarro 2009 and 10 in the Zanette 2008 trial.
S349: They were also at potential risk of bias from various aspects related to protocol design.
S350: Furthermore, among the three studies, only Di Lazarro 2009 provided mean baseline and follow-up scores with standard deviations.
S351: The other two studies merely presented summaries of statistical data which could not be pooled to perform a valid meta- analysis.
S352: Summary of main results See: Summary of findings for the main comparison.
S353: We found three randomised trials with 50 participants recruited; 20 with definite ALS and 30 with definite or probable ALS.
S354: Given the heterogeneity of the studies reviewed, combining our pre- specified outcomes among the studies was not possible.
S355: Moreover, these studies generally had small sample sizes and high drop-out rates, and as a result may provide unreliable evidence about the comparative eﬀects of rTMS for people with ALS.
S356: In Di Lazarro 2009, scores of ALSFRS-R and MMT were presented, with no significant diﬀerences between real and sham rTMS groups (MD -1.2; 95% CI -8.78 to 6.38 and MD 0.20; 95% CI -0.77 to 1.17).
S357: But in Di Lazarro 2006 and Zanette 2008, no raw numerical data were available.
S358: Two studies reported statistically significant improvement in the real rTMS group compared with the sham rTMS group.
S359: The first study (Di Lazarro 2006) showed slower deterioration in score of ALSFRS-R and MMT in the real-treatment group at six months (Table 3).
S360: The second study (Zanette 2008) claimed that rTMS may improve motor function (measured by maximum voluntary isometric contraction) and quality of life (QoL) (measured by SF-36) in people with ALS at four weeks (Table 3).
S361: However, there is still insuﬀicient evidence to reach conclusions on the eﬀectiveness of rTMS for the treatment of ALS.
S362: The treatment was reported to be well tolerated and seemed not to produce significant adverse events in any of the included studies, even in the study with long-term follow-up (12 months).
S363: The possible side eﬀects of rTMS observed in other reports, including headache, neck pain, tinnitus, dizziness, convulsions and memory loss, were not reported in the three studies included in this review.
S364: However, this finding should be interpreted with caution.
S365: In view of the small sample size and the methodological limitations, treatment with rTMS cannot be judged as completely safe.
S366: Overall completeness and applicability of evidence The data and the applicability of rTMS to treating ALS are questionable for several reasons.
S367: Firstly, small numbers of studies with relatively small sample sizes were included in our review.
S368: The search strategy of the review identified 296 citations, of which only three trials fulfilled all the selection criteria.
S369: The included studies used sample sizes of between 10 and 20 participants (median 16).
S370: Therefore the process of randomisation may not have been able to guarantee the equality of groups in all their variables.
S371: Secondly, the rTMS protocols and clinical pictures of the participants in the studies reviewed were heterogeneous.
S372: As a new technique for the treatment of ALS, there are no standard rTMS protocols that can be used for this disease.
S373: More exploratory investigations are warranted to examine diﬀerent combinations of rTMS parameters in the treatment of ALS, and how these could be integrated with Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation for the treatment of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or motor neuron disease (Review) Copyright © 2013 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S374: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 11 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S377: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews conventional therapies.
S378: Thirdly, the data for several important outcomes were lacking in some studies.
S379: Among the three included studies, only Di Lazarro 2009 provided detailed data of outcome measures before and afer the rTMS treatment.
S380: This meant that we could not do further analyses on the other studies.
S381: Quality of the evidence There is only limited evidence on the eﬀects of rTMS in people with ALS.
S382: Only three trials with 37 participants of the initially 50 eligible patients randomised were included in the review.
S383: Overall methodological quality of the included trials was low and is described in detail in the 'Risk of bias' table (Characteristics of included studies).
S384: For one study, sequence generation and allocation concealment were adequate (Di Lazarro 2009).
S385: For the other two studies, it was unclear whether these were adequate.
S386: Two studies included in this review were described as double-blind (Di Lazarro 2006; Di Lazarro 2009).
S387: However, when administering rTMS, it is impossible to blind the person who administers the technique, because they must have known whether each participant belonged to the treatment or the control group and used the right magnetic stimulator.
S388: Double-blind therefore refers to the participants, outcome assessors and investigators involved in other processes of the study and may increase the probability of biases related to the performance of the studies.
S389: Two studies in the review reported drop-outs adequately (Di Lazarro 2006; Di Lazarro 2009), with high rates in both intervention and control groups.
S390: The reasons for drop-outs in the trials included ALS-related problems, developing unrelated medical conditions, unwillingness to travel to the clinic for follow-up and protocol violation.
S391: Moreover, as the outcome measures were continuous variables, the trial investigators did not perform any intention-to- treat analyses at the end time-point of the studies, which may have aﬀected the final results.
S392: Potential biases in the review process We applied a very comprehensive search strategy to identify all potential studies and found three trials for inclusion, all of which were performed in Italy.
S393: However, research in this field has been performed outside Italy, as we noticed that experiments with rTMS for ALS have been widely conducted in other countries such as Japan and France.
S394: It is possible that some studies may not have been identified, if they were not published or if they were published in journals not indexed in widely accessible databases.
S395: We will include such studies in updates of this review if they are identified in the future.
S396: Agreements and disagreements with other studies or reviews Dileone and co-authors conducted a narrative review on rTMS for ALS (Dileone 2010), which summarised the results of four published studies.
S397: All studies were adequately described but no pooled analysis was performed due to the lack of suﬀicient data from these research studies.
S398: The authors concluded that rTMS is a safe and tolerable procedure for ALS patients and further studies should be pursued to determine if rTMS has applicable eﬀects on ALS, which is similar to the conclusions of our systematic review.
S399: However, we note that any potential benefits of rTMS need to be balanced with the demands of trial participation for ALS patients (Di Lazarro 2009).
S400: A U T H O R S ' C O N C L U S I O N S Implications for practice The included trials failed to use adequate methodology, examined only small number of participants, had incomplete outcome data and presented little raw numerical data.
S401: Low quality evidence from one small trial showed no significant diﬀerences between rTMS and sham rTMS for the ALSFRS-R scores and MMT scores at 12 months follow-up.
S402: There is currently insuﬀicient evidence of the eﬀicacy and safety of rTMS in the treatment of ALS from the included trials.
S403: Implications for research Further studies may be helpful if their potential benefit is weighed against the impact of participation in a randomised controlled trial on people with ALS.
S404: A rigorous method of randomisation should be used and the allocation adequately concealed.
S405: Missing outcome data should be avoided wherever possible and data should be analysed according to intention-to-treat principles.
S406: Some other important points relating to rTMS therapy should be taken into account before considering a new trial.
S407: With variation in frequency, intensity and duration of the magnetic field, further research is warranted to evaluate the potential eﬀicacy of diﬀerent protocols of motor cortex stimulation, particularly during the early stages of the disease when the progression rate is more pronounced (Dileone 2010).
S408: Some techniques and indicators could be used to explore neurochemical eﬀects of the stimulation to develop a more eﬀective protocol, such as magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) (Stagg 2009), diﬀusion tensor MRI (Sach 2004), the triple- stimulation technique (Magistris 1999) and BDNF production (Angelucci 2004).
S409: This type of human trial would likely require that intermittent rTMS be sustained over a long period of time until a dose-response curve was established, before proceeding to a larger scale clinical trial.
